content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 9, 1990

Dennis A. Barlow
Office of the City Attorney
City Hall
300 North "D" Street
San Bernardino, CA 92418

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-532

Dear Mr. Barlow:

You have written seeking advice on behalf of San Bernardino
city Councilmember Norine Miller regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Because your request is a general inquiry, we treat your request
as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c)
(copy enclosed).²

QUESTIONS

1. May Councilmember Miller purchase tickets for travel on
city business from a travel agency owned by her sister without
creating a conflict of interest under Section 87101?

2. Is a nonprofit private hospital a disqualifying source of
income to the councilmember when the councilmember holds a bond
issued by the hospital worth $10,000 which yields $650 in interest
annually?

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
Regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-90-532
Page 2


     3.  May the councilmember serve as a member of the board of
directors of a nonprofit private hospital without creating a
conflict of interest if the only compensation is four lunches
provided annually?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     1.  The councilmember may purchase tickets for city business
from her sister's travel agency.

     2.  Interest from a bond issued by a private nonprofit
hospital constitutes a potentially disqualifying financial inter-
est.

     3.  The councilmember may serve on the hospital board of
directors without creating a conflict of interest if her compensa-
tion is less than $250 in the twelve months preceding the deci-
sion.

                              FACTS

     1.  Councilmember Miller intends to purchase tickets for
travel on city business from a travel agency owned by her sister.
Presumably, the tickets will be paid for with city funds or
Councilmember Miller will seek reimbursement from the city.

     2.  Councilmember Miller holds a $10,000 bond from a private
nonprofit hospital.  This bond yields $650 in interest annually.
The hospital may come before the city council on matters such as
zoning variances and general plan amendments.

     3.  Councilmember Miller wishes to serve on the Board of
Directors of the same private, nonprofit hospital.  There is no
compensation paid except four free lunches annually.

                              ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating, or in any way attempting to use his official posi-
tion to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has
reason to know he has a financial interest.  An official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or any source
of income over $250 in the preceding 12 months.  (Section 87103.)

     1.  Travel Tickets:  By purchasing travel tickets,
Councilmember Miller is making a governmental decision because she
is obligating the city to a course of action and entering into a
contractual agreement on behalf of the city.  (Regulation
18700(b)(3), 18700(b)(4), copy enclosed.)  However, the decision
does not involve any financial interest of Councilmember Miller,
her immediate family, or a source of income to her.  "Immediate

Our File No. I-90-532
Page 3


family" means spouse and dependent children (Section 82029), and
therefore excludes her sister. Since the councilmember has no
financial interest in the decision, no conflict of interest arises
under the Act if she purchases travel tickets from the travel
agency owned by her sister.3

     2. Hospital Bond: The hospital is a source of income to
Councilmember Miller because it pays $650 in interest to her annu-
ally.4 (Section 82030(a).) Councilmember Miller must therefore
disqualify herself if the hospital is directly involved in a deci-
sion before the city council. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(1).)
"Directly involved" generally means the hospital appears before
the city council with some sort of request. (Regulation
18702.1(b), copy enclosed.)

     If the hospital is indirectly involved in the decision,
"materiality" is defined by Regulation 18702.5 (copy enclosed).
You must determine the nonprofit entity's gross annual receipts
and the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the decision to
determine whether councilmember will be required to disqualify
herself.

     3. Hospital Board of Directors: As a nonprofit entity, the
hospital is not a "business entity" for purposes of Section 87103.
(Section 87103, copy enclosed). Because the hospital pays its
members of the board of directors less than $250 annually, the
hospital would not be a source of income to Councilmember Miller
for purposes of the Act. (Section 87103(c).) Her membership on
the board would not, in itself, create a conflict of interest
because the hospital is nonprofit. (Section 87103(c).)
Therefore, neither the membership on the board nor the receipt of
lunches worth less than $250 would create a conflict of interest
under the Act should the city council be presented with a decision
regarding the hospital.


_______________________

     3 The Commission provides advice concerning the Political Reform
Act only. You may wish to consult with the Attorney General
regarding other provisions that may apply, such as Government Code
Section 1090.

     4 You stated that the hospital is private and that there is a
very limited market for the bond. If the hospital were a
governmental agency, the interest would not constitute a source of
income because interest from bonds issued by governmental agencies
is excluded from the definition of income. (Section 82030(b)(4).)
In addition, interest on a security registered with the Securities
and Exchange Commission is excluded from the definition of income.
(Section 82030(b)(5).) Since the bond has a limited resale
market, I am presuming it is not listed with the Securities and
Exchange Commission and thus does not come within the exception in
Section 82030(b)(5).

Our File No. I-90-532
Page 4


     In summary, the purchase of travel tickets from her sister
would not create a conflict of interest for Councilmember Miller,
nor would membership on the hospital Board of Directors. However,
the interest from the hospital bond does establish the hospital as
a source of income to the Councilmember and may result in a
conflict of interest.

     I trust this letter adequately addresses your questions. If
you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel
free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By: Peggy Bernardy
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:PB:aa

Enclosures
